title: Dyeing with Japanese indigo
subtitle: A direct plant dye from fresh Persicaria tinctoria leaves without a fermentation vat
excerpt: Japanese indigo (Persicaria tinctoria) can be used as a direct fresh-leaf dye without the fermentation vat process required by woad or dried indigo. This entry covers growing the plant, harvesting the leaves at peak colour, and dyeing wool or silk directly in the fresh-leaf juice.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from Japanese indigo growing and dyeing practice. Fresh-leaf Persicaria tinctoria dyeing is documented in Ehren Cruz and Japanese textile craft sources; the technique is distinguished from fermentation-vat indigo by its seasonal, ephemeral nature.

glossaryTerms.definition: A metallic salt that forms a bond between the fibre and the dye. Fresh-leaf Japanese indigo does not require a mordant for initial dyeing, though mordanting can improve washfastness.
For fresh Japanese indigo, the dye bath is the liquid produced by blending fresh leaves with water. Unlike fermentation indigo, no chemical vat is required.

content.text: Japanese indigo (Persicaria tinctoria) is grown as an annual in British gardens. The fresh-leaf technique does not require a fermentation vat; it works because the indigo precursor in the fresh leaves is in a form that bonds directly to the fibre when the leaf is physically broken and the juice contacts the wet fibre. The result is a soft, somewhat variable blue-grey to teal, distinctly different from the deep blue of a fermentation vat. The dyeing window is limited to the hours immediately after leaf harvest. An alum
is not required for colour uptake but can improve washfastness. The technique requires no specialist chemicals and produces a
that can go directly to compost once used.

attrs.title: Timing
attrs.body: Fresh-leaf Japanese indigo dyeing must be done immediately after harvest. The colour potential drops significantly within 2 hours of picking.

items.name: Fresh Persicaria tinctoria leaves (harvested at peak, before flowering)
Blender or large pestle and mortar for extracting the juice
Large bowl or basin (not a metal pot)

content.text: Step 1: Grow and harvest the plant

Sow Persicaria tinctoria seeds indoors in spring and transplant outdoors after the last frost. The plants grow fast and can be harvested from mid-summer onward. Harvest the leaves by cutting the whole plant back by half, just before flowering. The leaves contain the most indigo precursor just as the flower buds begin to form.

Immediately after harvest, place the leaves in a blender with 500 ml of cold water. Blend for 30 seconds until the leaves are fully broken down. Strain through a fine sieve or muslin cloth, pressing the residue firmly to extract as much green juice as possible. The dye liquid will be bright green but contains the blue indigo precursor.

Immerse the pre-wetted, well-squeezed wool or silk directly into the green juice. Squeeze and massage the fibre in the juice for 5 minutes, ensuring full contact. Remove the fibre and expose it to air by opening it out and swinging it gently. The colour will shift from green to blue as the indigo oxidises on exposure to oxygen. The process is rapid: the blue develops in about 1 to 2 minutes. Repeat the immerse-and-air sequence two or three times for deeper colour.

Rinse the dyed fibre in cool water until the water runs clear. The colour will be a soft blue-grey or teal. Dry in shade. The shade can be slightly improved by washing once in warm water with a small amount of wool wash after drying.
The spent leaf pulp and dye liquid can be composted. They contain plant material and water only, with no mordant chemicals.

items.problem: The colour is green rather than blue after airing
items.cause: The airing time was too short for the indigo to fully oxidise
items.fix: Open the fibre out fully and allow a full 2 minutes in air before assessing the colour. Fan the fibre to increase air contact.

items.problem: The colour is very pale despite using a large quantity of leaves
items.cause: The leaves were harvested after flowering or too late in the season
items.fix: Harvest in mid-summer, just before flowers appear. Leaves picked after flowering contain much less indigo.
